ontology.foodex2_description: Pork soup — used as the closest FoodEx2 category anchor for this pork-based broth dish with vegetables.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of pork ribs and winter melon soup is approximately 350g, comprising broth, pieces of winter melon, and one to two pork rib segments.

nutrient_source.title: Pork Ribs and Winter Melon Soup — Singapore HPB Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Pork Ribs and Winter Melon Soup (F06100034). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this canonical entry.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; winter melon / low-carbohydrate vegetable soup analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for pork ribs and winter melon soup. Winter melon (dong gua) is a very low-carbohydrate gourd vegetable with a GI estimated at approximately 15 based on analogous low-carbohydrate vegetable soups in the University of Sydney GI Database. With only 1.1g carbohydrate per 100g, the glycemic load per 350g serving is approximately 0.6, which is negligible. GI of 15 is a conservative analogous estimate consistent with low-carbohydrate vegetable-based broths.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content at 181mg per 100g; a 350g bowl delivers approximately 634mg sodium, which is 27% of the 2300mg daily limit.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to one small bowl and avoid adding salt during cooking, as sodium accumulates significantly with larger servings of this broth-based soup.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content of 1.1g per 100g with 0.6g dietary fibre; winter melon is a low-GI vegetable with estimated GI of 15.
type2_diabetes.guidance: This soup is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes as its negligible carbohydrate load will not raise postprandial blood glucose; however, total meal composition should be monitored when served alongside rice or other high-GI staples.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Pork ribs contribute saturated fat (estimated 0.4g per 100g) and the sodium content of 181mg per 100g is a concern for cardiovascular health at larger serving sizes.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this soup in moderate portions, choose lean pork rib cuts, skim visible fat from the broth before serving, and be mindful of cumulative sodium intake across the full meal.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pork ribs are a source of arachidonic acid (omega-6), which can promote pro-inflammatory cytokine production relevant to rheumatoid arthritis.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should limit frequency of consumption of pork rib-based dishes and consider substituting pork ribs with fish or chicken to reduce arachidonic acid intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pork Ribs and Winter Melon Soup (排骨冬瓜汤, Pái Gǔ Dōng Guā Tāng) is a traditional Singaporean Chinese clear broth soup made by slow-simmering pork ribs with winter melon (dong gua) in water, typically seasoned with salt and ginger. The soup is light, mildly savoury, and slightly sweet from the winter melon, which softens to a tender, translucent texture during cooking. It is a common home-cooked dish and hawker centre offering in Singapore, often served as part of a multi-dish meal alongside steamed rice. Nutritionally, the soup is very low in calories (20 kcal per 100g), low in carbohydrates (1.1g per 100g), and moderate in protein (1.6g per 100g) from the pork ribs. Sodium is 181mg per 100g, which accumulates with larger serving sizes. The dish is suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes due to its negligible glycemic load, and is borderline suitable for hypertension with portion control. The pork rib base contributes saturated fat and arachidonic acid, making it a moderate concern for cardiovascular disease and rheumatoid arthritis. Winter melon provides dietary fibre (0.6g per 100g) and calcium (12mg per 100g). The dish is gluten-free and dairy-free, and is not halal due to the pork content.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (F06100034), which is the canonical primary source for Singaporean dishes. The exact dish entry exists in the approved source with all core macros and key secondary nutrients reported. Confidence is high.
notes: saturated_fat_g value of 0.4g per 100g is estimated from the HPB source context and typical pork rib fat composition, as the HPB entry does not separately report saturated fat; this is documented as an adapted value. sugar_g value of 0.5g per 100g is consistent with the input record reference and typical winter melon sugar content. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1 as detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific prepared soup are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for F06100034 as of 2026-04-16. The dietary tag 'keto' from the input has been retained in classification context as the dish is very low in carbohydrates (1.1g per 100g). The dish is not halal as it contains pork. GI is an analogous estimate based on low-carbohydrate vegetable soup comparators from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for this specific dish was found in approved sources.
